title: Transcript Rack
desc: Yesterday's meeting left a drift of note tablets and crystal dust that the records rack cannot continue pretending is organized.

steps.text:
The summit records are kept in a side gallery on drying racks and shallow shelves because a hall of argumentative muges produces paperwork the way storms produce runoff. Clay note tablets, resonance sketches, amended proposals, and heavily annotated copies of older standards all wait here to be dried, stacked, and filed before they become indistinguishable from one another. Crystal dust from the main chamber has settled across half the rack, which is not helping the illusion of control.

The archive hand slides a stack of fresh blank slips onto the shelf. "Sort the note tablets onto the drying rack if you want the clay work," she says. "Or brush the crystal dust off the reference shelves if you prefer the books and standards. The summit's memory should not depend on luck."

options.label: Sort the damp note tablets onto the drying rack
Brush the crystal dust from the reference shelves

steps.text:
The note tablets are still soft enough to smear if handled carelessly and already full of sharp script explaining why the other twelve speakers were, apparently, mistaken in sequence. You separate them by session mark, stand them on edge in the drying rack, and leave space between each tablet so the air can do its work without turning adjacent arguments into one illegible compromise.

The archive hand checks the rack, reading only the top marks and not the contents, which is probably wise. "Good. Let them harden separately. That is more civility than the summit itself usually manages."

options.label: Set the last tablet in place and leave the rack breathing

steps.text:
The reference shelves hold older standards, harmonic tables, and enough previous amendments to suggest that certainty is a brief condition in Danian muge politics. Crystal dust has gathered in the ledges and page seams, leaving the older volumes sparkling faintly in a way nobody asked for. You brush the shelves clean, tap the dust from the binding edges, and return the heavier standards to their proper order so the next speaker can find precedent without excavating it first.

When you finish, the side gallery looks less like the aftermath of a clever landslide and more like an archive again. The archive hand runs a finger along one shelf and finds no grit. That seems to satisfy her completely. "Better. If a conclusion is ever reached in this hall, I would like there to be a readable record of the event."

options.label: Close the dust tin and leave the shelves legible
